q: How can you develop and test Azure Functions before deploying to Azure?
o: Run and debug them locally with the Functions tools
Only via the Dataverse form editor
why: The Azure Functions Core Tools/VS Code let you run and debug functions locally.

q: Which local file holds a function app's local app settings during development?
why: local.settings.json stores local settings/connection strings for local runs.

q: Decide whether each local-development statement is true.

rows.s: Local settings can be synchronized to the Azure app's application settings.
You can debug a function locally with breakpoints.
Local development requires a paid production plan.

why: Settings can be synced to Azure; local debugging is supported; no production plan needed to develop locally.
Every function has exactly one trigger that causes it to run.

q: What is the role of a binding (vs a trigger) in Azure Functions?
o: It declaratively connects input/output data to the function
why: Bindings connect data sources/sinks declaratively; the trigger starts execution.

q: Which are the possible binding directions?
why: Bindings are input (in), output (out), or in some cases inout.

q: Decide whether each trigger/binding statement is true.

rows.s: A function has exactly one trigger.
A function can have multiple input and output bindings.
A trigger and a binding are the same thing.

why: One trigger per function; multiple bindings allowed; triggers and bindings are distinct concepts.

q: Which programming models are shown as examples for writing functions?
o: C# isolated worker, Node.js v4, and Python v2
why: The module shows C# (isolated worker), Node.js (v4) and Python (v2) examples.

q: What is the recommended way for a function to connect to Azure services WITHOUT secrets?
o: Hardcoding a connection string in code
Storing the password in a label
why: Identity-based connections use a managed identity, avoiding stored secrets.

q: After enabling a managed identity, what else is required for it to access a resource?
o: Granting the identity the appropriate permission/role on the resource
why: You must grant the identity a role/permission on the target resource.

q: Decide whether each connection statement is true.

rows.s: Identity-based connections reduce secret management burden.
You configure the identity and then grant it permissions.

why: Managed identities remove secrets; you enable the identity and assign it permissions.

q: In the exercise, which tool is used to create the Azure Function?
why: The exercise creates an Azure Function using Visual Studio Code.
